data.title: ARK Invest adds new positions in VELO and SNPS
data.description:
ARK Invest disclosed on Tuesday that it has added a new position in Velo3D (VELO) to its ARK Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF (ARKQ), expanding the fund’s exposure as part of its ongoing portfolio rebalancing strategy.

Cathie Wood’s investment manager Ark Invest also has added a new  position in Synopsys (SNPS) to its $PRNT fund, the ARK Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF, signaling increased exposure to semiconductor software as part of the fund’s latest portfolio adjustments on January 20, 2026.

ARK Invest added 6,608 shares of Synopsys (SNPS), representing 4.59% of the Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF, valued at approximately $3.41 million.

 

More on Velo3D, Inc., ARK Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF, etc.

data.title: Alibaba joins hands with China’s top nuclear firm to power AI expansion - report
Teck Resources meets 2025 copper output guidance as Q4 copper sales lag; cuts Antamina zinc forecast
data.description:
Canada’s Teck Resources (TECK) said on Tuesday that it delivered 453,500 tonnes of copper production in 2025, supported by strong fourth-quarter performance across its operations, even as adverse weather and sea conditions temporarily weighed on Q4 sales.

Copper sales in Q4 2025 were lower than production, primarily due to a short-term build-up in inventory at Quebrada Blanca ((QB)) resulting from weather and sea conditions in December, which delayed shipments into early 2026, the company added.

Quebrada Blanca delivered a strong fourth-quarter performance, producing 55,400 tonnes of copper.

The miner's 2025 annual zinc in concentrate production of 565,000 tonnes, meanwhile, was at the higher end of the guidance range. Zinc in concentrate sales were 157,200 tonnes in the fourth quarter, with all Red Dog shipments completed early in the quarter, in line with the normal seasonality of sales.

Looking ahead to 2026, Teck Resources (TECK) said there have been no changes to its previously disclosed production guidance for 2026–2028, issued on October 7, across all Teck-operated sites.

The updates come as Anglo American (AAUKF) (NGLOY) and Teck Resources (TECK) are headed for antitrust approval in the European Union after the regulator signaled the absence of competition concerns for their $50 billion merger.

However, the company lowered its 2026 annual zinc in concentrate production guidance for Antamina to 35,000–45,000 tonnes from 55,000–65,000 tonnes, reflecting an updated mine plan finalized in the fourth quarter of 2025.

Asia stocks extend slump as Greenland tensions and Wall Street sell-off weigh
data.description:
Asia stock markets were mostly lower on Wednesday, tracking firmer U.S. futures after Wall Street’s major benchmarks suffered their steepest one-day drop since October 10, as renewed tariff threats from President Donald Trump against Europe stoked volatility concerns. 

Trump threatened tariffs on eight European countries over Greenland, prompting concerns that European funds might offload US assets in retaliation.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slid nearly 5% to below $89,000 on Jan. 20, wiping out year-to-date gains and tracking a broad risk-off move across global markets as heightened tensions between the US and Europe.

Gold extended its bullish run above $4,800 per ounce on Wednesday, setting a fresh record.

The South Korean won advanced to around 1,471 per dollar, rebounding sharply from a four-week low after investor sentiment improved on government assurances. South Korea’s exports grew 14.9% year-on-year to USD 36.36 billion in the first 20 days of January 2026, data from the Korea Customs Service showed on Wednesday. 

Japan (NKY:IND) slid 0.56%. The Japanese yen held steady around 158 per dollar on Wednesday but faced downward pressure after Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s proposal to cut the 8% sales tax on food raised concerns about the fiscal outlook.

Japan logged its highest-ever December tourist arrivals at 3.6 million in 2025, pushing total arrivals for the year to a historic 42.7 million and above the 40-million mark for the first time. 

Investors also eyed the Bank of Japan’s policy meeting later this week, where rates are widely expected to stay unchanged following a December hike.

 Meanwhile, investors are bracing for a snap election on Feb. 8, as Takaichi seeks to consolidate power and advance her expansionary fiscal agenda. 

China (SHCOMP) rose 0.16% to above 4,120, while the Shenzhen Component gained 1% to 14,300 on Wednesday, reversing losses from the previous session as investor sentiment in China improved, and the offshore yuan weakened to around 6.96 per dollar on Wednesday, snapping a two-session gain as the People’s Bank of China kept the daily fixing weak.

On the corporate front, China Vanke bondholders approved a plan to defer certain yuan repayments by one year, according to a filing. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.19% to 26,518 in Wednesday morning trade, attempting to break a four-session losing streak. 

India (SENSEX) fell 0.41% to 81,681 in morning trade on Wednesday, retreating for a third straight session. The Indian rupee fell toward 91.3 per dollar on Wednesday, hitting an all-time low amid persistent foreign capital outflows and heightened geopolitical uncertainty.

Australia (AS51) fell 0.39% to around 8,780 on Wednesday, extending losses for a third consecutive session. The Australian dollar held its recent gains to around $0.673 on Wednesday, trading near a two-week high.

The Westpac–Melbourne Institute Leading Economic Index for Australia edged up 0.1% month-on-month in December 2025, following a flat reading in the previous month. 

In the U.S. on Tuesday, all three major indexes ended deep in red, with the S&P 500 down 1.9%, the Dow dropped 1.7%, and the Nasdaq sliding 1.9%. This was due to increased trade risks from President Trump's tariff threats.

U.S. stock futures steadied on Wednesday after the major averages slumped in the previous session: Dow +0.18%; S&P 500 +0.29%; Nasdaq +0.27%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Trump's Air Force One bound for Davos takes a U-turn
data.description:
The Air Force One plane carrying U.S. President Donald Trump to the World Economic Forum in Switzerland was forced to turn back late Tuesday due to what the White House called a “minor electrical issue” shortly after takeoff from Joint Base Andrews.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters aboard that the crew identified a minor electrical issue after takeoff and prudently decided to turn around and return to the base.

Air Force One landed back at the base at 11:07 p.m. ET. The president and those accompanying him will board a new aircraft and resume the trip to Switzerland, Leavitt said.

The technical glitch will likely delay Trump’s highly anticipated speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, scheduled at 8:30 a.m. ET Wednesday, shortly after landing.

The reporters aboard said that the lights in the press cabin briefly went out after takeoff, but no explanation was offered.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
